html:
Oscar II, Oscar Fredrik, born January 21, 1829, at Stockholm Castle in Stockholm, and died December 8, 1907, also at Stockholm Castle, was King of Sweden from 1872 to 1907, King of Norway from 1872 to 1905, and Duke of Östergötland from 1829 to 1872. He was the third son of Oscar I and Josefina, and the brother of Karl XV. Oscar married Sofia of Nassau in 1857. He himself used the spelling "Oscar," and after his death, the spelling practice has varied between this and "Oskar."

Oscar II reigned during a period when Sweden underwent industrialization and rapid technological development, and when the union between Sweden and Norway weakened before ultimately dissolving. This period is sometimes referred to as the "Oscarian era." Oscar was the third of four brothers in a family of five children. When the eldest brother, King Karl XV, had no heir (his only son died in infancy in 1854, and his daughter lacked inheritance rights), and the middle brother, Prince Gustaf, died in 1852, it was Oscar who inherited the throne upon Karl's death in 1872.

== Early Life ==

Oscar II's christening took place on January 28, 1829, at 6:00 PM in the King's Guard Hall (from 1845, known as the White Sea). The ceremony was officiated by Archbishop Carl von Rosenstein, and present alongside him were Bishop Esaias Tegnér of the Växjö diocese, Bishop Pehr Thyselius of the Strängnäs diocese, Order Bishop Johan Olof Wallin, and Court Chaplain Johan Jacob Hedrén. Erik Gustaf Geijer was also present at the baptismal font. Immediately after the christening ceremony, 128 cannon shots were fired in two volleys.

=== Childhood and Education === Queen Josephine aimed to raise the princes to be resilient and not spoiled. The princes and princess shared the same dining room, bedrooms, and playroom in the eastern wing of the palace. Like his siblings, Oscar was raised under the supervision of Countess Christina Ulrika Taube. The royal children were expected to exemplify "an impeccable and morally upright life," as stated in Queen Josephine's instructions to the tutor. From 1833 to 1837, Christopher Jacob Boström served as his teacher of practical philosophy. As Oscar and his brothers were also Norwegian princes, the children needed to learn to read and write Norwegian. Their Norwegian tutor was Otto Aubert, who also taught them mathematics and gymnastics. Lessons took place between eight and twelve in the morning and between four and six in the afternoon. However, Aubert suffered from tuberculosis and died in 1838. He was replaced by "the father of Swedish gymnastics," Pehr Henrik Ling, who unfortunately passed away the year after Aubert. The children received an unusually high-quality artistic education from leading music and art teachers from the Royal Academy of Fine Arts. Adolf Fredrik Lindblad was their music teacher. In addition to arts and music education, the children learned to dance quadrilles and waltzes. Occasionally, the children were even involved in creating their own learning materials; for example, Oscar drew maps of England during geography lessons. In history classes, the children learned about Swedish history, as well as the duties of a prince.

When Oscar was fifteen years old, and his older brothers were nineteen and seventeen, they went to Uppsala University to study, where he studied mathematics. His father had arranged for accommodation, and Oscar and his brothers tried to attend most of the lectures given by Per Daniel Amadeus Atterbom and Erik Gustaf Geijer. During their time in Uppsala, Oscar began his military career, as all princes were assigned regiments corresponding to the provinces they governed. Prince Oscar was assigned to the Royal Navy and had enlisted at the age of ten on the frigate Götheborg.

=== Career in the Navy === Oscar trained as a naval officer. He obtained his officer's diploma in July 1845, and from 1846 to 1847 he participated in a year-long voyage aboard the frigate Eugénie in the Baltic Sea and the Mediterranean. He became a captain at the age of 23 and ended his career as a rear admiral in the navy during the Crimean War, at the age of 26. He not only wrote technical books on the subject, such as "On the Debarkation Troops of the Archipelago Fleet" and "Proposal for Exercise Regulations for His Majesty's Landing Troops," but also poetry; under the pseudonym Oscar Fredrik, he published the collection of poems "From the Memories of the Swedish Navy" in 1858. He was also an inspector of the Swedish military schools. On December 13, 1848, he was appointed the first honorary member of the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences. From 1865 to 1872, he served as chairman of the Military Society in Stockholm. Oscar became a lieutenant general in the army on December 1, 1858.

Oscar also participated in several debates regarding the navy, both with the then-reigning king and the then-Minister of the Navy, Baltzar von Platen. The latter had proposed a bill to the Riksdag (Swedish Parliament) for the years 1850-1851, which Oscar disapproved of. There were major debates, also in the 1860s when von Platen returned as Minister of the Navy.

=== A Unified Scandinavia === During his time in Uppsala, Oscar and his two older brothers, Gustav and Karl, along with several others, had the idea of creating a unified Nordic kingdom under a single king. The then-reigning king, Oscar I (Oscar II's father), also showed interest in this.

How certain are we about this?

People from the 20th century are extremely well-documented. Uncertainty rarely concerns facts but rather interpretation and evaluation, which can change over time and depending on the observer.
tldr: The text describes the life of King Oscar II, from his upbringing as a prince to his time as king of Sweden and Norway.
lattlast:
Oscar II was born in 1829 and ascended to the throne in 1872. He grew up in a large family where he received a thorough education, including music, art, and mathematics. As a prince of Norway, he also learned to read and write Norwegian during his childhood.

As a young man, he studied at Uppsala University and began working in the military. He became a highly knowledgeable naval officer and even wrote books on military subjects. He was also interested in art and poetry, publishing poems under his own name.

During his reign as king, he ruled over Sweden and Norway during a period of significant change driven by new technology and industry. He was also involved in efforts to promote cooperation within the Nordic region and dreamed of a unified Nordic kingdom.
